docs.title: Welcome
docs.text: Asadbek Ruziev Datasheet as part of  Exploration Device Project for  Crop S.C.O.U.T.

docs.title: Update: March 5, 2026
docs.text:
Team 202

Submission: February, 23, 2026

docs.title: Project Summary
docs.text:
• I am responsible for mobillity of the rover project. We are team of 7 engineering students who are designing explorative rover in 4 months. Please, take a look in the process of our design through different tabs in the team report. fF you would like to learn more about other team member's subsystem, access team report and follow link for each member's website.
  • To get more information about our team work follow Team 202 Team report.

docs.title: My Contribution
docs.text:
• Our team is focused on making land based explorative rover project that drives to different places and uses its sensors to perform measurements.
  • I am responsible for mobility of the rover using ESP32. I will be using 4 motors with wheel attached. Motors will be working through the motor driver to move the robot from the distance through WiFi.

To review the details listed of the material used to construct the subsection, you can review it in the "Team" section of the datasheet.

To review the details of the data sheet, hyperlinks are provided below: 1. Requirements 2. Block Diagram) 3. BOM 4. Component Selection 5. Schematic 6. PCB Design 7. Appendix

docs.title: Module Requirements
docs.text:
This section of the website outlines the requirements for the proper operation of my subsystem. The table includes threshold and target measures for each requirement. The rover's mobility is key, and ensuring that the motors and wheels support the upper body is crucial. Each requirement is important for the proper operation of my subsystem, ensuring it not only works properly but also meets EGR 314 criteria. Motor drivers require 9V, so my subsystem needs an additional power source. I have also included an additional column to show if the measure stretch requirement is met. The target measure is ideal for my motor, PCB, and power system.

Requirement description Measure of threshold Target measure Stretch requirement (Y-N) Surface-mounted, 3.3 V switching regulator 3.2 Volts Stable 3.3 Volts No Surface-mounted microcontroller for mobility control 1 PIC18F or ESP32 ESP32 No Wireless communication through UART Able to send or receive data with at least a wireless communication subsystem Send and receive Wi-Fi Data to MQTT to all subsystems Yes Consumption of power Motors, motor driver, and PCB do not heat up too much Meets power budget guidelines No Low-voltage DC motors 4 Brushless DC motors 2 brushless DC motors can hold and move the upper body of the rover No Motor driver L298N motor driver No overheating in the test window No Shared power for motor Module runs well with pin header Consistent power without need to change source No The motor moves forward and back All wheels turn on flat surfaces No slipping and works well on hard surfaces too No Motor turns right and left 2 motors on the left and 2 on the right turn together and move, staying in the same place The steering system works, and the 2 motors work with better-controlled movement Yes 4 Wheels Wheels are good enough to hold the rover Wheels operate well and have stable alignment No Mechanical constraints Fits within a decided shape Clean mounting of motors to the PCB No

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Purpose of having block diagram is to help visualise my subsystem so that you can understand the workflow of code and schematics better. I have included 2 motors that are going to be parallel on the right. 2 motors on the left will also work together. I have planned to make the rover working with 2 motors together on each side, then making chassis as my stretch goal. This block diagram clearly shows how power, control and data will be floweing through my subsystem and to make the whole rover system work. Mobility of the rover is crucial to successfull launch of the project.

docs.title: Asadbek's Block Diagram
docs.text: Showing my screenshot of the block diagram here

docs.title: Impoortant Things to mention are following:
docs.text:
• ESP32-S3-WROOM-1
  • 4 Motor Drivers
  • 4 Motors
  • Voltage regulator: 1 barrel jack is going to be used twice through shared power for the team and inidividually. I will use 2 voltage regulatorsand one of them will be to convert voltage into the 3.3 V and 6V.
  • Daisy chain connectors through 8-pin header connectors Connector In (4×2), Connector Out (4×2) and UART communication.
  • USB
  • Power levels: Unregulated power levels are going to be 9V for the motors, but the input to ESP 32 is 3.3V. For logic-level peripherals of my subsystem's ESP 32 I will use 3.3 volts.
  • Actuator: I will be using 4 same DC motors for smooth operation of the rover.
  • Team connections: I will be connecting my ESP32 to Caleb's board to oeprate better through RX and TX
  • Power source: barrel jack for the motors are main sources of power.

SPI UART serial communication protocols are just alternative communication ways to connect to motors actuators sensor. All WiFi is done by esp32.

docs.title: Component Selection
docs.text:
Module’ selected major components:

Motors: I have decided to choose the 2nd option because it is relatively cheap compared to the first. Apart from that, a gearmotor can work with a lower voltage. I have used this motor on many of my projects, and it has never caused issues.

Component Pros Cons FIT0489-D Gearmotor 40 RPM 12V turbo metal Price: $18.9 Works on hard surfaces Very nice torque for high load Low power consumption Very costly Heavier than most gearmotors and has very low RPM Need more power and hard to work with surface mount motor drivers Adafruit Gearmotor 200 RPM 3-6V DC Price: $2.95 Has high RPM Has special holes for mounting to the rover wheel Works well with lower voltage levels. Can’t always handle high load 1528-2607-ND GEARMOTOR 120 RPM 3-6V BI-METAL Price: $4.5 Bi-metal gearbox Stronger than a simple gear motor Good price for its usage Low RPM Bit costly

3.3 buck voltage regulator Final selection: LM2575 Rationale:

Component Pros Cons LM2575D2T-3.3R4G Price: $2.16 Very clear datasheet Good cost Up to 40V input voltage Has issues with Amperage Higher cost AP63203WU-7 Price: $0.71 Wide input range 2A output current Protection inside from thermal shutdown Noise of the switch UA78M33CDCY Price: $1.07 Low cost Only input and output needed Low efficiency with more than 6V supply LM2596S-3.3-ND Price: $8.76 Works well with 3A Input voltage operation up to 40 V Simple design for soldering Fixed at 150 kHz Too costly for my subsystem

Motor driver. This motor driver will be used to make 4 of my motors move in different directions and to help control their speed. I am going to choose the 2nd option for my motor driver.

Component Pros Cons BH6766FVM-GTR Price: $1.02 Suitable for small systems TLE9201SGAUMA1TR-ND Price: $3.77 Can use 2 motors Speed control with enable 1 and enable 2 Very high cost compared to the other 2 options IFX9201SGAUMA1 Price: $3.55 Simple design Short circuit protection Careful PCB layout Higher cost than many motor drivers Two devices would be required, so a bigger PCB space is needed

Notes: All parts are from Digikey or other reliable part providers to ensure compatibility and availability of datasheet.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This schematic is designed to support 4 motors, motor drivers, ESP32 subsystem. I will also going to be sending and receiving data through TX and RX of the 8-pin headers. Symbols were sourced from the Digikey website.

Figure 1: Showing my schematic

docs.title: 1. Microcontroller interface
docs.text:
• ESP32-S3-WROOM-1
  • UART daisy chain interface
  • USB for programming
  • Boot, reset buttons ESP32 microcontroller is included in this schematic.

docs.title: 2. Motor Driver
docs.text:
• SPI configuration (SCLK, SDI, SDO, NSCS)
  • PWM → PWM input for speed
  • DIR → direction
  • DIS → enable/disable
  • VSO I am also going to use 4 motor drivers. The motor driver is surface mount H-bridge. Out1 and Out2 allows to control the speed and input comes from the GPIO pins of ESP32, while output goes to motors.

docs.title: Shared SPI
docs.text:
• GPIO11 → SI on all drivers
  • GPIO13 ← SO on all drivers
  • GPIO12 → SCK on all drivers Since, I am going to use 4 motors together, I will have 3 shared SPI pins that go to the same ESP32 GPIO as above.

docs.title: 3. Motors
docs.text: I am going to use 2 headers for each of my motors as it can not be surface-mount. In total, I will be using 4 motors that will be working with 4 motor drivers. * 200 RPM 3-6V DC Gearmotor

docs.title: 4. Power Management
docs.text:
• 9V barrel jack for motors
  • LM2575D 3.3 Voltage regulator
  • Fuse for the barrel jack for safety

docs.title: Appendix - Controller Table for the ESP32
docs.text:
Microctronoller selection

Microctronoller selection

I have decided to choose the ESP32 because it has low power consumption and supports both WiFi and Bluetooth. I can control the motors via Wi-Fi on my phone. I will send a signal to Christopher and receive a response from Jacob via Bluetooth. I will be sending and receiving data from Caleb’s board. Table1: ESP32-S3-WROOM-1-N4 diagram

Component Pros Cons ESP32-S3-WROOM-1-N4 Wifi and Bluetooth enabled Easier to control the motors with a motor driver Budget friendly Can’t make more more complex subsystem High power usage PIC18F47Q10 $2.63 Stable control applications Good peripheral control Less power will be needed Costly Does not have wifi and Bluetooth mounted Limited memory ESP Info Answer Help Model ESP32-S3-WROOM-1-N4 Include the entire part number (leave off any letters at the end that specify the package type) Product Page URL ? Found on Espressif.com ESP32-S3-WROOM-1-N4 Datasheet URL URL Do not paste links directly into the table. Use a link ESP32 S3 Datasheet URL Datasheet link Has more detail on functions ESP32 S3 Technical Reference Manual URL [] Has details on I/O multiplexing, USB, and others Vendor link Link Code Examples ? url(s) for libraries on github or other sites related to the microcontroller and your planned peripherals External Resources URL(s) ? Search on Google and YouTube for other resources for each specific microcontroller. Unit cost $5.06 Find on Digikey, Jameco, MPJA, or octopart Absolute Maximum Current for entire IC ? Find in the microcontroller datasheet Supply Voltage Range 0.3/3.3/3.6 V Min / Nominal / Max / Absolute Max, as found in datasheet Absolute Maximum current (for entire IC) 1500 mA as found in datasheet Maximum GPIO current (per pin) 40mA as found in datasheet Supports External Interrupts? Yes as found in datasheet Required Programming Hardware, Cost, URL USB Micro, $0.78 [Link] (https://www.digikey.com/en/products/detail/jae-electronics/DX4R005JJ2R1800/3903229?s=N4IgTCBcDaIGwHYAMBaMiCsKCMKByAIiALoC%2BQA) as found in datasheet Module # Available Needed Associated Pins (or * for any) UART ? ? ? external SPI* ? ? ? I2C ? ? ? GPIO ? ? ? ADC ? ? ? LED PWM ? ? ? Motor PWM ? ? ? USB Programmer ? 1 ? ...

* The ESP32-S2 has multiple SPI interfaces, but some are for internal use
